sentences.text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]
My name is David Mandel, D-A-V-I-D M-A-N-D-E-L.
No.
But I've been-- my Hungarian name was Tibor.
And my Jewish name is Mordechai David.
And I go by David Mandel without the middle name right now.
My birthday is August 28, 1929.
And that makes me 66 years of age.
I was born in Munkacs, Czechoslovakia.
It was called Mukacevo under the Czech rule.
Before 1918, it was Hungary.
It was called Munkcas.
And then, in 1938, the Hungarian part of a Nazi Germany's ally took it back from Czechoslovakia .
And it was again-- was called Munkacs.
M-U-N-K-A-C-S.
My father's name was Isaac Mandel.
And we were a family living there for generations in Munkacs.
My mother was born there.
My grandmother was born there.
My immediate family consisted of five brothers and one sister.
We lived a very, very happy, close family life in Munkacs.
And we went to Hebrew school as kids.
We went to cheder.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were very, very happy.
Yes.
My father was-- had a sort of a mini-factory.
Wood-turning.
He was making all kinds of spinning wheels, weaving machines for the peasants.
And we manufactured parts for furniture companies, and so on.
We had a-- my father was a very hard worker.
We had a lucrative type of a business.
And we lived-- we lived a very, very normal life.
That was prior to 1938.
And in 1938, I remember when the war-- when the Hungarians took over our area, I was only nine years old.
And we didn't think much of it.
I remember when the Hungarian army marched into Czechoslovakia.
As a kid, I was sort of jubilant I saw a parade.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that's when the difficulties started with us.
And I started listening to the grown-ups.
And even though I was nine years old, I started understanding the seriousness of our situation.
The city of Munkacs was just a beautiful city nestled in the Carpathian Mountains, with the nice, clean streams.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We enjoyed playing soccer.
And we had some friends.
They were non-Jewish friends.
Of course, we associated mostly with Jewish kids.
I remember having gone to this-- to the chedar and also to the Hebrew school.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And then things started changing all of a sudden.
Yeah.
OK.
Oh, my mother's name was Zisel.
My grandfather's was-- name was [INAUDIBLE] Zev.
My grandmother's name was Mirel.
My other grandmother was Rifka.
My brother's names were Shmuel-Zvi-- he was a couple years younger than I.
And then I had Becalel-Tavji, Yehudah-Arjeh.
And my older brother-- I forgot.
His name was Jacob Moshe.
And we all went by Hungarian names as well.
My sister's name was Vera.
She was the youngest.
We were five brothers and one sister.
I was about nine years old.
I was born in 1929, and she was born in 1938.
And your oldest brother, how many years apart were they?
Two years older.
He was born in 1927.
So the-- let me see.
He was 11 when she was born.
And I guess my family wanted a girl.
And they finally got one.
And she was a beautiful child.
And in your home, how did you observe the holidays?
Well, most of us were pious people.
We observed the-- the holidays.
We observed all the kashrut.
We observed the Yiddishkeit.
We-- we just-- it was a family tradition that was only-- not only a family tradition, but the city of Munkacs, I would say, consisted of about maybe 35,000 population of 20-- 35,000 people.
And, I would say, about maybe 30% were Jewish.
We probably had about 15,000 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jewish people there.
And my father belonged to the Mizrachi.
There was a Zionist organization, but that was the religious part of it.
And we just followed in the footsteps of my father and my grandfather.
My father was a hardworking man.
My mother was busy keeping a kosher, nice home.
And her aim in life was to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] earn deeds doing mitzvah.
She was helping out the needy, sometimes cooking for them and feeding them.
And well, we-- we just-- we were God-fearing people.
Very.
Yes.
Most of them were religious people.
And-- and most of them-- most of them, I would say 90%, as far as I can recall.
I started to.
The non-Jewish kids had nothing to do with-- with us anymore.
I remember one time [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one of my brothers and my sister, we were in a-- out of town on a country road.
I don't know what we were doing, whether we were going to a neighboring village.
And I remember some non-Jewish kids were tending cows.
And as we went by, they-- I heard the word, 'zsid,' 'Jews.' And they came over for no reason whatsoever.
And they started whipping us.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's when I noticed there is a difference.
Before, we got along fine.
So there was something instilled into children as well as they probably would listen to the elders in their own community.
All of a sudden, we were no good.
And we, at the time, couldn't understand it.
I didn't understand it.

But as things went on, they finally-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] out of a close-knit family, the way I was brought up, I noticed the problems and the new rules that were being made against the Jewish people.
And so, I was very concerned.

I think from then on in, we-- instead of becoming-- being normal, like normal kids wanting to do the things, we started-- I started thinking ahead and starting to fend for-- for my life and also for the lives of my younger brothers and especially my sister.
Well, we-- we were-- we became a little bit more serious.
And we stayed away from anyone that was not Jewish.
We didn't want to run into any kind of problems.
We didn't want to create any problems for my dad.
Children that age, they're very proud.
And they look up to their parents, that they can handle almost anything.
And so we just adhered to the-- to the things that my-- my parents told us.
So we started being withdrawn.
Later on, they-- they-- what they did is they were weakening our midst.
Anyone at a certain age, young men up to a certain age were being [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] called in-- into service.
But it wasn't the army.
It was a work battalion.
They called it in Hungarian, 'munkaszolgalat.' And they were sent away, probably to the front.
And I understand now we know that they were given shovels and picks.
And they had to haul the ammunition and-- and-- and-- instead of giving them guns.
And most of those men never came back.
That was already in 1941, '42. 1941, '42.
And my father was called in in 1942.
In other words, they were weakening our midst.
Not in 1938 right away, but it happened-- it started in 19-- end of 1939 and 1940.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kids could not go beyond the sixth grade in school.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jewish men had to have permission to travel certain distances.
It was mild rules.
But it was-- it was-- they-- they just advanced the rules, you know, to 1943, '44, where they formed ghettos.
And we had to stay in ghettos.
But [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I don't want to get ahead of myself because the-- the-- the-- the real cruelty started in 1943, the end of 1943 and 1944.
Yeah.
Yes.
Yeah, they had restrictions.
But it was mild restrictions at a time, in comparison to what we had later on.
Well, I remember that I went to my father's factory, and I helped work.
We all learned trades.
My father became-- My-- my brother, my older brother, became a dental technician.
I helped out my dad's factory.
Also, Jewish kids, at the age of twelve-- at the age of 12, had to give their time in service to the city.
We were called the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], 'the youth,' where we had to-- the Jewish kids had to report in the morning.
And we had to go to these municipal places and sweep the grounds, and to sporting arenas and clean up.
In other words, they just used us practically for slave labor.
I remember, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as a kid-- that was on Fridays.
We would-- we would-- they would keep us sometimes till the Sabbath would set in.
And we still didn't get home.
And Sabbath was something that we all looked forward to.
It was-- we still had our mother's cooking.
And we went to bathe and clean and put out new clothes, and so on.
It was a way of life.
And when I think back, that they kept us-- and sometimes on purpose-- that's one of the things that I-- I remember.
And it was slowly-- slowly they just-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when I think back, my-- my childhood was taken away at the time.
Well, they hoped that things would improve.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they might have heard stories that things are taking place in other parts of the world, perhaps in Poland.
And I don't know-- news bits were trickling in.
But to this day, I just don't understand how people would not believe that-- and not have known that, even by that time in 1941, '42, that families were being killed.
We had no-- we had no idea that that was taking place.
So I guess maybe my parents knew that it can be worse.
And they were hoping.
Everybody lived by hope.
1940-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it-- we just-- we just resorted to that type of a life.
And we sort of lived like second-class citizens, I suppose.
In 1942, '43, my father was taken away.
Yes.
Before the ghetto my father was taken away, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] leaving my mother home with six children.
And my father was put into this work battalion.
And at a time, they had leniency because my father was a sugar diabetic.
And they sent them to a hospital like about 60 kilometers away from Munkacs.
And I remember one time, my brother and I-- in 1942, I was 13 years old, 12-- 12 years old-- my brother and I ran away from home to see him.
And we went 60 kilometers away to-- to visit my dad.
But luckily, they sent my dad back.
And my dad came home.
And my dad was with us.
And as he came back, they-- they came out with some new rules that everyone had to prove citizenship in the area.

And since my grandfather, my father's father, [INAUDIBLE] Zev, came originally from Poland in the 1800s sometime as a kid-- because he came to the area from Poland, my father was not deemed a citizen.
And, therefore, my mother was not deemed a citizen, and my brothers and myself.
So we had to-- I remember my father feverishly was going through all kinds of offices, trying to get the necessary papers.
And while that was going on, in 1942, they came and they-- came at night.
They took people away.
In 1942, they took people away who were not citizens.
But my father had a friend at the police headquarters, who notified him that tonight they're coming to get you.
And I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went into hiding.
Well, I remember middle of the night I had to carry my little brother, who was about maybe eight years old.
My brother carried my sister.
They were still sleeping.
And we went in the backyard.
We had like, bomb shelters.
And we hid out there.
And then, at night, we were dispersed.
I remember sleeping in stables, on the attic with straw.
I remember going out the outskirts, and other people were hiding us out.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was a time that I was supposed to be Bar Mitzvah.
[PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] Actually, to this day, [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I've never been Bar Mitzvahed.
Anyway, they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one time, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this one man, who was in charge of our yard, who was not Jewish, caught me on the street.
And he says, the police have been [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] looking for you.
And he grabbed me by my arm, and he took me to city hall.
And I was going along, and as I felt his [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] grip loosening, I ran away.
But eventually, we thought it was safe.
I remember my mother and I went home to the house.
And they came.
I guess we were reported then.
And I remember in 1942, they came and they arrested us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, actually, 1942, there we were.
My father, my mother, my sister, we were in jail.
And I remember first time I was ever at the police headquarters in jail, they had wooden bunks and cement floors.
And we were there.
And then, as kids, they sort of let us run around in the hallways.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they stopped taking us.
But later on, of course, we found out those people that they were taken away never came back.
And I remember I went to a lecture a few years ago.
There was a Christopher Browning, who did all kinds of research on the Holocaust.
And I asked him, I told them about this-- what happened to me in 1942.
And I asked him whether he knew anything about it.
He says, oh, yes.
Those people that were taken away were taken to the front in Poland, Podolsky-Kamenets.
And eventually those are the ones-- they had to-- they were shot in a common grave.
And-- that's-- we escaped that fate.
We-- And then we went back and we-- my father went back to work.
And I suppose they knew what the end is going to be.
So we just lived with the rest of the Jewish population in the city of Munkacs until 1943.
Early 1944 they created the ghetto.
In jail?
Yeah, they-- well--
--we had to-- we had to show up, you know?
Once they had one member of the family, we all had to show up.
So we all--
I think about three, four days.
Not too badly.
Not too badly.
I remember-- this was-- you know, the jail keepers were not the Germans.
They were the Hungarian.
And they were just following orders.
Yeah.
They let us go home.
We went back to home.
And our hiding was-- was-- came to an end.
